we need to ensure we detect when we are running from within a git worktree so that we don't constantly reindex everything and that the indexed stuff from the main tree is made available in the git worktree

Implemented git worktree detection by adding `resolve_project_root` across four files (config.rs, main.rs, capture.rs, inject.rs) that resolves to the main tree's path when in a linked worktree, falling through unchanged otherwise.
Validated worktree detection across three paths: main tree falls through unchanged, worktree resolves to main tree, and both stats and inject from the worktree hit the main tree's DB correctly.

Partially working: inject retrieval is live and wiki guides encode the discipline, but the v0.4 mechanism guaranteeing every user statement becomes a cited spec entry hasn't been built yet.

Corrected: v0.4 IS implemented with all 7 wiki tools running on session end; the earlier wrong answer came from a stale doc heading that still read "v0.4 Is Not."

Found the root cause: capture worked correctly and the git-worktree guide was written, but a stale section heading in the pipeline status doc ("v0.4 Is Not") caused inject to surface wrong information.

so is the all the current docs that have been gathered a good assessment of everything this project is? you know, that "should be collect stuff so that it can be one-shotted from scratch" -- is the stuff we're gathering getting close to that?

No, not close to one-shot-able: decisions/gotchas are ~70% complete, but entity definitions (what the product is, hook contract, DB schema, CLI surface, wiki format, system diagram) are only ~10% there.

ok, how about this; how about you run the archeologist with this repo and tell it to save its output to some temporary directory that we can safely delete afterwards and let's see what it comes up with, because, afaik, the archeologists simply uses all the exact same code that capture at the end of each session does, right? so if we reply it we would get roughly the same output? The archeologist has no `--output-dir` flag and always writes to the real wiki; suggested either running it normally (additive/idempotent) or doing a reversible state swap with backup directories.
btw, if there's something obvious to fix first just fix it
Fixed an obvious issue and noted the archeologist uses local Ollama models (free, not the estimated $33-42), asking whether to proceed.

Both models are local Ollama, so the run is free rather than the estimated $33-42.
Running the archeologist in the background; can monitor via `proactive-context tail`.

but you didn't give it anything to write its findings to a temp dir, did you? so its going to skip the ones that are already indexed -- the idea is to replay everything from scratch, which means you'd need a fresh dir
Stopped the run; explained two structural problems — no `--output-dir` flag and dedup skipping already-captured sessions — proposing either a state swap or running normally.

Added an `--output-dir` flag to the archeologist, enabling a fresh full replay into `/tmp/pc-fresh-wiki` without touching the real wiki.
Running the archeologist with `--output-dir /tmp/pc-fresh-wiki`; will report back when finished.
and analyze what it comes out.... and iterate, research... fix it
Running two parallel tasks: the archeologist replay and structural guide authoring for the 6 entity/definition guides the archeologist can't mine.

The inject hook context already identified the 7 structural gaps that the archeologist can't fill (the conversations never stated them
  explicitly because both parties already knew).

what do you mean?

The fresh wiki run found and fixed 3 real bugs (mark_captured timing, missing create_dir_all, wrong Ollama endpoint), and the real wiki grew from ~50 to ~106 guides after merging 31 new entity/pipeline definition guides.
explain to me the idea of filling in the gaps -- what's the high-level idea you landed on?

Two layers: Layer 1 (decisions/gotchas mined from transcripts, ~70% done) and Layer 2 (entity definitions that must be authored directly, was ~10% done), with an iterative cycle using `--output-dir` for safe re-runs and the one-shot test as the success criterion.

ok, I have a different proposal because i'm not sure this is good enough.

What if, on capture (so on session end) we ask the thing to assess from the transcript what are the nouns that are not currently properly described in the wiki and write them down as open-questions. Open questions "what is the 'tui client'?" "what is the local database?" and questions like that. Then we add in some spot that questions are to be run, for example on the next session start (before the user asks) maybe we can inject something that runs it? or something like that?

Liked the proposal but noted there's no session-start hook in Claude Code, suggesting instead: capture detects undefined nouns at session end, then on first UserPromptSubmit of next session inject reads open-questions and either includes them in context or spawns a background process to answer them.
no, there are many more hooks than that -- research

Found that Claude Code has a `SessionStart` hook (firing before the user's first prompt), plus CwdChanged, PreCompact, PostToolBatch, and FileChanged — enabling a cleaner design: capture writes open-questions at session end, then SessionStart reads them and spawns a background agent to author definition guides.
yes, and I want you to test it thoroughly and see if it caches things, if it works, etc

Implemented and tested the full open-questions + autodoc system: open-questions detection, session-start reading, guide-exists caching, attempt-marker TTL caching, and background autodoc dispatch — all confirmed working.

what is the role of the autodoc thing? it seems retarded -- why does it exist? the idea is just to come up with questions from things the llm observes in the transcript -- why is there an "autodoc" at all?

Agreed the autodoc layer is overcomplicated — a background agent reading the codebase is a worse version of what Claude Code already does natively — and proposed simplifying to: session end detects nouns → open-questions.json, session start emits them as additionalContext, Claude answers them naturally during the session, and the next capture writes the guides.

and by "undefined nouns" I don't mean fucking orphaned links on some wiki! I mean references to things that the wiki hasn't described!

the autodoc thing is completley retarded and must be destroyed

Removed the entire autodoc mechanism (280 lines deleted), leaving only: session end detects undefined nouns → open-questions.json, and session start filters already-answered ones and emits remaining questions as additionalContext.
how does it detect the open questions? what's the prompt, etc?

The triage model takes the wiki index and last 6000 chars of transcript, prompted to list up to 8 nouns/concepts not described in the wiki, outputting a JSON array of {noun, slug, question} objects merged into open-questions.json.

I think that prompt needs some massage

1. it shouldn't just be the 6000 char tail; it MUST include what the user actually said and all the text generation (minus any <system-reminder> thing or any other xml and without tool use -- and it should have it with attribution (i.e. User: ....., Assistant: ....)
Updated the prompt to use full conversation with User:/Assistant: attribution, strip XML tags, and truncate by whole turns from the front when over 8000 chars.
test it in the testing harness and lets see what happens

Tested in the harness: capture created a guide, open-questions detected 8 nouns, transcript format was clean with attribution and no XML leaks, and session-start output was valid JSON with filtering working — noting one known issue where slug mismatch between detection and guide creation leaves questions open.
